quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Primary battery"?

options.text: A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)
Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases
The temperature and pressure at which all three phases coexist in equilibrium (water: 0.01°C, 611.7 Pa)
An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Primary battery" is defined as: A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Secondary battery"?

options.text: The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea
Relates free energy to cell potential; n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
A collision with enough energy (≥ Ea) and correct molecular orientation to break bonds and form products
A rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is reversible by applying an external voltage (e.g., lead-acid, Li-ion, NiMH)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Secondary battery" is defined as: A rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is reversible by applying an external voltage (e.g., lead-acid, Li-ion, NiMH). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Fuel cell"?

options.text: An electrochemical cell that converts fuel (typically H₂) and oxygen directly to electricity and water; high efficiency, no combustion
ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
Equilibrium constant for weak acid dissociation: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; larger Ka = stronger acid
The change in concentration over a finite time interval: Δ[A]/Δt; decreases over time as reactants are consumed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Fuel cell" is defined as: An electrochemical cell that converts fuel (typically H₂) and oxygen directly to electricity and water; high efficiency, no combustion. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Corrosion"?

options.text: Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3; each successive proton is harder to remove because it leaves a more negatively charged anion
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
The electrochemical oxidation of metals by environmental exposure (water, O₂, salt); iron rusting: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻
Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Corrosion" is defined as: The electrochemical oxidation of metals by environmental exposure (water, O₂, salt); iron rusting: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cathodic protection"?

options.text: Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
Preventing corrosion by making the metal structure the cathode; achieved with a sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg) or impressed current
FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges
Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cathodic protection" is defined as: Preventing corrosion by making the metal structure the cathode; achieved with a sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg) or impressed current. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)"?

options.text: Primary battery
Half-reaction method
Atomic number (Z)
Alloy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Primary battery".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is reversible by applying an external voltage (e.g., lead-acid, Li-ion, NiMH)"?

options.text: Stoichiometric coefficient
Secondary battery
Gibbs free energy (ΔG)
Conjugate acid-base pair

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Secondary battery".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An electrochemical cell that converts fuel (typically H₂) and oxygen directly to electricity and water; high efficiency, no combustion"?

options.text: Fuel cell
Phase diagram
Homogeneous catalyst
Second law of thermodynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Fuel cell".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The electrochemical oxidation of metals by environmental exposure (water, O₂, salt); iron rusting: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻"?

options.text: Corrosion
Standard free energy of formation
Kinetic molecular theory
Factors affecting rate

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Corrosion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Preventing corrosion by making the metal structure the cathode; achieved with a sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg) or impressed current"?

options.text: Indicator
Polarizability
Cathodic protection
Mole

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cathodic protection".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Batteries and Corrosion is accurate?

options.text: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.
Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.
Equilibrium is dynamic (reactions continue but net change is zero).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Batteries and Corrosion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Batteries and Corrosion?

options.text: Primary batteries (alkaline: Zn/MnO₂, 1.5V) are single-use.
If a reaction is reversed, ΔH changes sign; if multiplied by a factor n, ΔH is multiplied by n.
Crystalline solids have ordered lattice structures (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic), while amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order.
The choice of method depends on the properties of the mixture components and the purity required.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Batteries and Corrosion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Batteries and Corrosion?

options.text: Balanced equations provide mole ratios for calculating how much product forms from given reactants, or how much reactant is needed.
Secondary batteries (lead-acid: Pb/PbO₂, 2V; Li-ion: LiCoO₂/C, 3.7V) are rechargeable by reversing the cell reaction with applied voltage.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.
For redox reactions, the half-reaction method balances electron transfer in acidic or basic solutions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Batteries and Corrosion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Batteries and Corrosion?

options.text: Fuel cells (H₂/O₂ → H₂O) convert chemical energy directly to electrical energy with high efficiency.
Activation Energy and Catalysts are central to understanding and controlling reaction rates.
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Batteries and Corrosion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Batteries and Corrosion?

options.text: Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.
The rate constant k varies with temperature.
Corrosion is an unwanted galvanic process where metals are oxidized; prevention methods include galvanizing, cathodic protection, and coating.
Activation Energy and Catalysts are central to understanding and controlling reaction rates.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Batteries and Corrosion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Batteries and Corrosion?

options.text: Industrial Haber process using iron catalyst to lower Ea for N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ from 230 kJ/mol (uncatalyzed) to ~60 kJ/mol, making ammonia synthesis economically viable
Electric vehicle batteries: Tesla uses lithium-ion cells (LiNiCoAlO₂ cathode) delivering ~250 Wh/kg, enabling 300+ mile ranges
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield
Soil pH affecting nutrient availability for plants: most crops grow best at pH 6.0-7.0; acid rain lowering soil pH reduces crop productivity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Batteries and Corrosion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Batteries and Corrosion?

options.text: Quality control in pharmaceutical manufacturing: acid-base titrations determining the exact concentration of active ingredients in drug formulations
Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar
Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Batteries and Corrosion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Batteries and Corrosion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Lithium-ion batteries exploiting Li's small atomic radius and low ionization energy for high energy density in lightweight portable electronics
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)
Road salt (NaCl) lowering the freezing point of water to prevent ice formation on roads—effective down to about -9°C (ΔTf = iKfm, i = 2 for NaCl)
Ship hull protection: zinc sacrificial anodes bolted to steel hulls corrode preferentially, protecting the more expensive steel from saltwater corrosion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Batteries and Corrosion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Batteries and Corrosion?

options.text: Quality control in pharmaceutical manufacturing: acid-base titrations determining the exact concentration of active ingredients in drug formulations
Water treatment using selective precipitation: adding Na₂S to remove toxic heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Hg²⁺, Cu²⁺) as insoluble sulfide precipitates (very low Ksp values)
Mass spectrometry separating isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio to determine elemental composition in forensics and pharmaceutical analysis
Electric vehicle batteries: Tesla uses lithium-ion cells (LiNiCoAlO₂ cathode) delivering ~250 Wh/kg, enabling 300+ mile ranges

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Batteries and Corrosion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Batteries and Corrosion in practice?

options.text: Hydrogen fuel cell vehicles (Toyota Mirai): H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O generating electricity with only water as byproduct; zero emissions at point of use
Household acids and bases: vinegar (acetic acid, Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵), lemon juice (citric acid), baking soda (NaHCO₃ acts as a weak base)
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Batteries and Corrosion.
